credit.author: Veres Deni Alex
credit.source: Sony Film Simulation Recipes V.pdf + veresdenialex.com

recipes.name: Vektro 100
recipes.film: Original creation, no film ancestor - high contrast, cool tones, subtly balanced color
recipes.tip: Made for bold, iconic images - try it for portraits or fashion too if you like an edgier look.

recipes.name: Senova Light
recipes.film: Author original - airy pastel light with a gentle glow
recipes.tip: Has separate day (8000K B2-M2) and night (2900K A5-G1) white balance.

recipes.name: Fuji 400H
recipes.film: Discontinued pro color negative (2004-2021) - cool tones, soft skin, a wedding favorite
recipes.tip: Prefer warmer tones? Simply raise the temperature by 300-500 Kelvin.

recipes.name: EvPro+
recipes.film: Original all-rounder profile, not based on a film stock - soft gradations, strong in low light
recipes.tip: Adjust Black level to taste: around -9 in daytime, -2 at night; raise it for a faded, dreamy look.

recipes.name: Ektar 100
recipes.film: Ultra-fine-grain color negative (2008) - the most saturated C-41 film, loves bright light
recipes.tip: Shines in bright light - expose correctly and the colors get richer and more vibrant.

recipes.name: Kodak Gold
recipes.film: Consumer color negative of the 80s-90s - warm, golden, the look of family albums
recipes.tip: Go hot with white balance - around 7800K with A5-G3 or A7-M1 - and expose for your subject.

recipes.name: Portra 800
recipes.film: High-speed daylight color negative - fine grain, natural skin tones, thrives in low light
recipes.tip: Easy to underexpose by accident - set +1 to +2 EV for clean, well-balanced shadows.

recipes.name: Kodak Portra 400
recipes.film: The gold-standard portrait color negative - natural skin tones, soft balanced color
recipes.tip: Very forgiving - slight under- or overexposure won't hurt; a great pick for beginners.

recipes.name: Kodak Portra 160
recipes.film: Low-speed Portra - creamy colors, smooth contrast, classic soft Kodak vibe

recipes.name: Kodak Color Plus 200
recipes.film: Budget classic since 1972 (Kodacolor II) - fun, vibrant, gritty grain like Kodak Gold
recipes.tip: Pairs beautifully with a black mist diffusion filter for a soft, film-like glow.

recipes.name: Kodak Ultra Max 400
recipes.film: Consumer all-rounder negative (2007, replaced Gold 400) - the look of early-2000s snapshots
recipes.tip: The author typically underexposes by 0.3-0.7 stops in daytime; skin roll-off stays nice even overexposed.

recipes.name: EktaChrome
recipes.film: Kodak's revived slide film - cool-clean color, editorial feel
recipes.tip: Expose to the right for warm, saturated color; underexposing shifts the whole image toward magenta.

recipes.name: Kodachrome 64 V1
recipes.film: Legendary slide film (1935-2009) - deep saturated color that documented an entire century
recipes.tip: For the vintage look, raise temperature 200-300K and nudge the color filter gently toward Blue-Magenta.

recipes.name: Kodachrome 64 V2
recipes.film: Golden Age 1950s Kodachrome - golden skin, velvet blue cast, Saul Leiter's palette
recipes.tip: Uses a floating Kelvin value: run 3900K for cooler tones, up to 5000K to emphasize golden skin.

recipes.name: Kodachrome 25
recipes.film: Slowest, sharpest Kodachrome - crisp slide color of 60s-70s reportage

recipes.name: Sony Eterna
recipes.film: Eterna mood rebuilt for Sony - soft cinematic base for stills or video

recipes.name: Fuji Eterna
recipes.film: Fuji cinema stock - flat, calm, low-contrast look made for storytelling
recipes.tip: Great video default; leaves room to grade.

recipes.name: Classic Chrome
recipes.film: Fuji's homage to old photojournalism chrome films - muted tones, relaxed cyan-leaning blues
recipes.tip: Try early morning or late afternoon light to bring out its nostalgic, cinematic feel.

recipes.name: Astia
recipes.film: Fuji slide film tuned for skin - soft contrast, gentle faithful color

recipes.name: Velvia Pro
recipes.film: Fuji slide film (1990) - vivid saturation, deep contrast, the landscape legend
recipes.tip: Punchy options like Velvia Pro thrive in bright, sunny conditions.

recipes.name: Fuji Provia
recipes.film: Fuji color reversal film - fine grain, vivid yet balanced color reproduction
recipes.tip: Very forgiving - the author overexposes +0.5 to +1 stop; around 4500K is the temperature sweet spot.

recipes.name: Provia RX
recipes.film: Author's richer Provia - slide color with extra bite
recipes.tip: Distinct from Fuji's Provia sim - orange skin against a deep blue cast; great beyond portraits too.

recipes.name: Acros X
recipes.film: Fuji Acros B&W film recreated on Sony - with X/Y/R/G filter variants, nearly indistinguishable

recipes.name: Acros XY
recipes.film: Acros with a yellow filter - slightly darker skies, natural skin
recipes.tip: Yellow-filter look: the classic all-round B&W choice.

recipes.name: Acros XR
recipes.film: Acros with a red filter - dramatic dark skies, glowing skin
recipes.tip: Red-filter look: strongest sky separation, great with clouds.

recipes.name: Acros XG
recipes.film: Acros with a green filter - brighter foliage, textured skin tones
recipes.tip: Green-filter look: best for greenery and male portraits.

recipes.name: Fortia 50
recipes.film: Fuji's rarest slide film - maximum saturation, made for flowers
recipes.tip: Made for experimenting - over- or underexpose and shift temperature for wild color shifts.

recipes.name: Sony Classic Negative
recipes.film: Sony-only twist on Fuji's Classic Neg - warm nostalgia, faded shadows, a focus on reds
recipes.tip: Indoors it can run too warm - drop the temperature by 700-1500K to balance ambient light.

recipes.name: Nostalgic Neg
recipes.film: Original recipe inspired by faded family prints - pink, orange and olive, not a film replica
recipes.tip: Not an emulation of Fuji's Nostalgic Neg - the magenta cast gives shots a magical, aged feel.

recipes.name: Classic Cinema
recipes.film: Vintage Hollywood print feel - teal-leaning shadows, warm skin
recipes.tip: AWB White Priority makes it easy under changing light.

recipes.name: Oktar
recipes.film: Author original - dense, saturated warmth made for nature
recipes.tip: Don't box it into nature shots - the author encourages using it on your own terms.

recipes.name: Zero Mute
recipes.film: Author original - restrained, desaturated calm with soft tones

recipes.name: CineChrome
recipes.film: Author original - chrome-slide color with a cinematic curve
recipes.tip: HLG3 gamma: turn Gamma Assist off.

recipes.name: Pro Color
recipes.film: Author original - punchy commercial color, crisp and confident
recipes.tip: HLG3 gamma: turn Gamma Assist off.

recipes.name: Blue Velvet / Cinestill 50D
recipes.film: Cinestill 50D by day - fine grain, gentle blue-leaning daylight color
recipes.tip: A daylight-balanced recipe that shines on sunny days; add a diffusion filter for extra punch.

recipes.name: Cinestill 800T - Nightime Recipe
recipes.film: Tungsten cine film beloved for neon-soaked night scenes and red halation
notes: Check this playlist for the basic workﬂow https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL-gfcmjtW3nup_6nXvu8JTn3obVpU2-o-&si=rv0vwEauDuwe
recipes.tip: Made for night-time use - one of the author's picks for low-light shooting.

recipes.name: Cinestill X
recipes.film: Eastman Double-X - the timeless cinema B&W of noir and Oppenheimer
recipes.tip: Keeps its deep tonal range even when overexposed; formerly named 'Cinestill X', now 'Cinestill X - BwXX'.

recipes.name: T-Max 100
recipes.film: Kodak's modern fine-grain B&W - clinical sharpness, smooth tones

recipes.name: Delta 3200
recipes.film: Ilford's high-speed B&W - big grain, deep mood, made for dim light
recipes.tip: The author recommends it for portraits and wedding photography.

recipes.name: Kosmo Pan
recipes.film: Kosmo Foto Mono 100 - Ukrainian-rolled classic pan B&W, soft and filmic

recipes.name: Chroma Fade
recipes.film: Faded-memory B&W with a whisper of color response left in
recipes.tip: Highlights max out around 75% with faded shadows - lean into it for soft, aged, melancholic scenes.

recipes.name: Kodak Tri-X - 1600 Pushed
recipes.film: Tri-X pushed two stops - punchy contrast and heavy honest grain
notes: Turn Off Noise Reduction for JPG Images
Shoot ISO 1600 or higher to increase Granularity
recipes.tip: Shoot ISO 1600+ and turn JPG noise reduction off for real grain.

recipes.name: Kodak Tri-X 400
recipes.film: The most famous B&W film ever - soft flat contrast, mid grain
notes: Turn On Noise Reduction for JPG Images & Set to Low
Shoot ISO 1600 or higher to increase Granularity
recipes.tip: Shoot ISO 1600+ with noise reduction set to Low for the grain.

recipes.name: Neo Max
recipes.film: Author original B&W - modern deep blacks with smooth midtones

recipes.name: Ilford HP5
recipes.film: The workhorse of British B&W - forgiving, gritty, endlessly classic

recipes.name: MidRed Infra
recipes.film: Infrared-inspired B&W - bright foliage, black skies, surreal contrast

recipes.name: DreamNeg
recipes.film: Author original - hazy dreamlike negative with lifted shadows

recipes.name: Agfa Precisa
recipes.film: Agfa slide film loved for cross-processing - cool teal-blue twist
recipes.tip: Very forgiving in mixed light - the author never changed temperature between scenes; a diffusion filter softens highlights.

recipes.name: RedScale Ultra
recipes.film: Film loaded backwards - everything bathed in orange-red fire
recipes.tip: WB needs a custom reading off a teal card (>9900K).

recipes.name: Zetra 100
recipes.film: Author original - balanced neutral negative with quiet character
recipes.tip: Color shifts go unnoticed in neutral scenes - it shines in vibrant, colorful landscapes.

recipes.name: Rose Spectra
recipes.film: Author original - rosy warmth flattering for skin and travel light
recipes.tip: Too subtle for lovers of loud color and deep contrast - pick it when you want warmth and quiet emotion.

recipes.name: Asteroid City - Kodak Vision T200
recipes.film: Wes Anderson's pastel desert - Kodak Vision3 200T pushed to pastel
recipes.tip: S-Log gamma: turn Gamma Assist off.

recipes.name: Ayon 200
recipes.film: Author original - deep yellow cast, like memories left in the sun
recipes.tip: Turns skies green and deepens reds - use it to make ordinary scenes feel sun-drenched and dramatic.

recipes.name: Phenix Harman
recipes.film: Harman Phoenix 200 - experimental new film, bold halation-like warmth
recipes.tip: Shadows and highlights clip hard and are tough to recover - skip it if you like re-grading colors in post.

recipes.name: Phenomena
recipes.film: Author original - saturated, warm, slightly surreal color fantasy

recipes.name: Crimson
recipes.film: Author original - red-leaning warmth with rich magenta cast

recipes.name: Acidnom
recipes.film: Author original - acid-bright palette with twisted color shifts
recipes.tip: Use it to see familiar surroundings in a completely new, fantastical way.

recipes.name: Estra 500
recipes.film: Author original - subtle dreamy haze with muted pastel color
recipes.tip: Plays nicely with soft backlight or overcast skies - gentle highlights without harsh contrast; great for weddings.

recipes.name: Arista Edu 100
recipes.film: Budget student B&W film - honest mid-contrast, classic grain feel

recipes.name: X-Tarr
recipes.film: Author original - the deepest blacks in the pack, near-void contrast
recipes.tip: Embrace the crushed blacks - it suits shots where the subject is the story; skip it if you want soft, airy tones.

recipes.name: MagicSpice
recipes.film: Author original - golden all-rounder with a spicy warm kick
recipes.tip: One of the most versatile recipes in the pack.

recipes.name: Amarelo 30D
recipes.film: Author original - yellow-gold daylight negative, sunny and dense
recipes.tip: A love-it-or-hate-it look - best in natural light; golden hour makes the sun-kissed skin glow even more.

recipes.name: GAF 500
recipes.film: Discontinued 70s high-speed film - coarse, warm, unmistakably retro

recipes.name: Vespera - Nightime
recipes.film: Author original for night - tungsten glow kept warm and cinematic
recipes.tip: Built for artificial light; try it at dusk too.

recipes.name: Veniliqum - Night
recipes.film: Author original for night - bold color under neon and streetlights
recipes.tip: Great under sodium-vapor street lights, which are incredibly hard to balance out.

recipes.name: Gold Luxe - Nighttime
recipes.film: Author original - gilded night look; also lovely in daylight
recipes.tip: Author: nighttime recipes like Gold Luxe look fantastic during the day too.

recipes.name: Kodak Gold
recipes.film: Kodak Gold: warm consumer negative, rich golden tones with a vintage twist
recipes.tip: Nostalgic recipes like Kodak Gold shine in golden-hour light.

recipes.name: Kodachrome 50s
recipes.film: 1950s Kodachrome slide film: vibrant, bold colors and fine grain

recipes.name: Fujifilm Fortia 50
recipes.film: Fuji Fortia 50: ultra-vivid slide film with rich contrast for max impact
recipes.tip: Made for bold landscapes and vibrant scenes that demand maximum visual impact.

recipes.name: Kodak Vision3 200T
recipes.film: Kodak Vision3 200T cine stock: balanced contrast, subtle shifts, timeless tone

recipes.name: Phoenix Harman
recipes.film: Harman Phoenix: unique filmic look, soft shadows and warm undertones

recipes.name: Kodak Portra 400
recipes.film: Kodak Portra 400: soft contrast and warm, natural skin tones
recipes.tip: Ideal for portraits and lifestyle photography.

recipes.name: Cinestill 800T
recipes.film: CineStill 800T: tungsten-balanced with a subtle glow, cyan-leaning night look
recipes.tip: Its coolness counters warm sodium-vapor street lighting at night.

recipes.name: Classic Cuban Negative
recipes.film: Cuban Negative: warm, dusty vintage travel-documentary tones by @osanbilgi
recipes.tip: Prefer fixed WB? Use 4600K with A7. A diffusion filter adds an organic film feel.

recipes.name: Provia
recipes.film: Fuji Provia: balanced natural colors, moderate contrast, great all-around
recipes.tip: Sony runs brighter than Fuji: underexpose 0.3-0.7 stops to match.

recipes.name: Velvia
recipes.film: Fuji Velvia: punchy, saturated slide film for landscapes and vibrant scenes
recipes.tip: Thrives in bright, sunny conditions.

recipes.name: Astia
recipes.film: Fuji Astia: softer contrast and flattering skin tones, made for portraits

recipes.name: Classic Chrome
recipes.film: Fuji Classic Chrome: muted vintage look with subtle desaturation and contrast

recipes.name: Pro Neg Std
recipes.film: Fuji Pro Neg Std: soft contrast, natural skin tones, clean understated look

recipes.name: Classic Negative
recipes.film: Fuji Classic Neg: muted tones and lifted blacks, nostalgic moody street vibe
recipes.tip: The author's favorite Fuji simulation - the one he finds truly looks like real film.

recipes.name: Nostalgic Neg
recipes.film: Fuji Nostalgic Neg: warm tones, soft contrast, old family-photo glow

recipes.name: Eterna
recipes.film: Fuji Eterna: low contrast, subdued colors, gentle highlights - cinematic calm
recipes.tip: Great for video or storytelling stills.

recipes.name: Sony VV Film Recipe
recipes.film: Author's warm faded film recipe on the Vivid base, also shared by Sony

recipes.name: Sony FL Film Recipe
recipes.film: Warm amber film look on the FL base: rich colors and plenty of contrast
recipes.tip: FL is arguably Sony's best Creative Look; great starting point for own film emulations.

guides.title: White Balance for film simulations
guides.essence: Recipes are daylight-balanced (~5500K), so use fixed Kelvin + color filter instead of AWB for consistent color. When light changes, add small offsets on top of the base recipe rather than chasing neutral - the intentional color cast IS the look. Adjust by eye when skin tones or vibe feel off.
steps: Outdoors in daylight: shoot the recipe as published, no changes
Incandescent bulbs: drop 1000-2500K or shift 2-6 toward Blue
Tungsten studio light: drop ~2000K or shift 4 toward Blue
Fluorescent: add 500-1000K (or 2A) plus 1-2 Magenta to kill green
Indoor natural light: drop ~300K or 1 toward Blue
Overcast/shade: add 500-1000K or shift 2 toward Amber
Kelvin maxed out? 2 points on the Blue-Amber filter ~ 1000K
Sodium-vapor night: use a cool recipe (CineStill 800T) or a low-saturation AWB recipe

guides.title: Sony setup: custom buttons, formats, Memory Recall
guides.essence: Map White Balance, Picture Profile, ISO and focus to custom buttons so recipe tweaks take seconds in the field. Shoot RAW+JPEG (or RAW+HEIF) since recipes bake into JPEG/HEIF only, and store full recipe + WB + exposure combos in Memory Recall slots to swap looks instantly.
steps: Assign custom buttons: White Balance, Picture Profile, ISO, focus area
Shoot RAW+JPEG or RAW+HEIF; the RAW stays untouched as backup
Enter the recipe in a PP slot, then set its Kelvin + color filter
Save the whole setup to Memory Recall (slots 1-3 are permanent; MR slots vanish on card format)
Underexpose 0.3-0.5 stops to protect highlights; S-Log-based recipes need +1 stop instead
Recipe looks off? Check white balance first, then adjust by eye
New light source: add/subtract the Kelvin difference from 5500K to the recipe
Kelvin scale maxed: ~1.75 steps on the Blue-Amber filter ~ 1000K

guides.title: Picture Profiles and the PP1-PP11 slots
guides.essence: The Picture Profile menu (Exposure/Color > Color/Tone) is the deeper tool for film recipes: gamma, black level, black gamma, knee, color mode, saturation, color phase, per-channel color depth, and detail. Factory mapping: PP1-PP6 ready-to-use gammas (Movie, Still, ITU709, Cine1/2), PP7 S-Log2, PP8-PP9 S-Log3, PP10 HLG, PP11 S-Cinetone - but any slot can hold any custom recipe.
steps: Pick gamma for contrast: Movie/Still = punchy, Cine1-4 = soft filmic, S-Log = flat/faded base
Set Black Level: lift for fade on contrasty gammas, drop to -10/-15 on Cine gammas
Refine shadows with Black Gamma range and level
Set Knee to ~75-80% with slope +1/+2 for soft highlight roll-off
Choose a Color Mode that fits the look, then set saturation
Use Color Phase to rotate all hues, Color Depth to darken/brighten each channel
Set Detail to -7 for a soft, organic, analog sharpness
Save the slot and recall it via your custom PP button

guides.title: Calibrate any Sony body to the recipes
guides.essence: All recipes are calibrated on the a6700; older bodies (a7III era) lean cold/magenta with lower saturation. Correct on top of the recipe using Kelvin, color filter, and Color Phase. Reds on old sensors often cannot be fully fixed - sacrifice reds to keep the rest of the palette right.
steps: a7RV, ZV-E1, a7CII: virtually identical to a6700; at most a tiny Amber/Green nudge
a7CII and RX100 VI/VII: raise Kelvin 100-200 or push filter ~0.5 toward Amber
a7III: Color Phase +4 and filter A3-G1; accept slightly pink reds
a7SIII: bump saturation; raise Color Phase +1/+2 if yellows go green
Underexpose rather than overexpose: blown highlights push yellows and skin toward green
Use Color Depth to deepen colors and avoid color clipping in highlights

guides.title: Color-match multiple Sony cameras
guides.essence: Pick your newest body as the color reference, identify each camera's dominant cast (warm vs cold, magenta vs green), then counter it with Kelvin, color filter and Color Phase added on top of the recipe. Work in small increments, judge skin tones first, and eyeball until it feels right.
steps: Choose the newest sensor as reference (recipes assume the a6700)
Diagnose the cast by checking reds, yellows, greens and blues
Cold/magenta cast: raise Kelvin, filter +G0.25-0.5, Color Phase +2
Warm/yellow-green cast: lower Kelvin ~300, push filter toward Magenta
Skin too pink: Phase -1/-2; too orange or green: Phase +1/+2
Adjust in 100-200K and 0.25-filter increments; recheck frequently, don't over-correct

guides.title: S-Log: when and how to shoot it
guides.essence: S-Log maximizes dynamic range for grading: use S-Log2 on 8-bit bodies (overexpose +1.5-2 stops), S-Log3 on 10-bit bodies (expose normally: white card ~61%, skin ~52%). Stay at base ISO, fix white balance manually, and skip Log entirely for fast turnarounds, low light, or run-and-gun.
steps: 8-bit camera: S-Log2 (PP7), overexpose +1.5-2 stops, skin ~55-60% IRE
10-bit camera: S-Log3 (PP8/PP9), expose to Sony's normal levels
Stay at base ISO (~640-800); never use Auto ISO in Log
Set a fixed custom white balance; flat footage magnifies WB drift
Control exposure outdoors with ND filters, not aperture compromises
Judge exposure with histogram + zebras (~94% highlights, ~70% skin)
Protect highlights first; lifting shadows beats recovering clipped skies
Skip Log for quick delivery, dim scenes, or fast-changing light

guides.title: Creative Look vs Picture Profile
guides.essence: Creative Looks are quick 10-slot JPEG styles (contrast, highlights, shadows, fade, saturation, sharpness, clarity + a basic WB filter) - pick them for convenience, beginners, video-fast workflows, and the unique FL/IN/SH/VV2 color modes. Picture Profiles add black level, gamma, knee, color modes, Color Phase and per-channel Color Depth - pick them for accurate or complex film emulation, better highlight roll-off and skin tones. RAW files are unaffected by Creative Looks; PP gammas do slightly affect RAW brightness.

cheatsheet.a: Every recipe uses a fixed Kelvin - set it once and leave it. Never AWB: it re-decides color on every shot.
Nudge the color filter: toward Amber to warm up, toward Blue to cool down. 2 filter points = roughly 1000K.
Drop the recipe's Kelvin by 1000-2500K, or shift the filter 2-6 toward Blue.
Add 500-1000K plus 1-2 toward Magenta to kill the green cast.
Add 500-1000K, or shift 2 toward Amber, to keep the recipe's warmth.
Daylight recipes turn orange under lamps. Switch to a night recipe: CineStill 800T, Vespera, Veniliqum or Gold Luxe.
Underexpose 0.3-0.5 stops to protect highlights - blown highlights push skin toward green. S-Log recipes want +1 stop instead.
Save full setups (recipe + WB + exposure) to Memory Recall 1-3, and map Picture Profile and White Balance to custom buttons.
Shoot RAW+JPEG. Recipes bake into the JPEG only - the RAW stays untouched as a backup.

clInstall: Set image format to JPG or RAW+JPG; looks bake into JPEG only
Press MENU, open the Exposure/Color tab, then Color/Tone
Select Creative Look and pick the base slot the recipe uses (ST, PT, NT, VV2, FL, IN...)
Press center/right button to open the look's custom settings
Enter contrast, highlights, shadows, fade, saturation, sharpness, sharpness range, clarity
Set white balance and color filter from the recipe (Color Tone is a basic global filter)
Press OK to save, back out, and shoot
